content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA—THE RESOURCES AGENCY

CALIFORNIA COASTAL COMMISSION
45 FREMONT, SUITE 2000
SAN FRANCISCO, CA 94105-2219
VOICE AND TDD (415) 904-5200

PETE WILSON, Governor

[seal]

JAN 21 9 03 AM '92

January 14, 1992


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

RE: Request for Advice Concerning Fees for the San Onofre Nuclear
Generating Station Resource Mitigation Project

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

Your office has recently provided written advice to a number of public
officials to address the circumstances under which fees paid to a public
agency and used to retain additional employees or consultants may not be
considered a "source of income" for those persons so retained. (Savage Advice
Letter No. 90-662a, Alperin Advice Letter No. 1-91-324, Egan Advice Letter No.
1-91-111.) In these advice letters, your office concluded that the fees will
not be considered a source of income to employees or consultants retained by
the agency if certain criteria are satisfied. These criteria are set forth in
the Alperin letter and section 1870.6 of the FPPC's regulations. Subsequently, an urgency measure became law which amended the Political Reform Act to
add new Government Code section 87103.6, which provides, in relevant part,
that a person who makes a payment to a governmental agency to defray the costs
of processing an action shall not be a "source of income" to a person retained
by the agency (Chapter 887, Statutes 1991).

We request advice with respect to the application of these criteria to the
hiring of scientific consultants by the Coastal Commission as part of the
independent monitoring and adaptive management of the San Onofre Nuclear
Generating Station (SONGS) marine resource mitigation projects required by the
Coastal Commission as a condition of approval of permit No. 183-73. The
mitigation projects are designed to address the damage occurring to marine
resources from the operation of SONGS. The mitigation projects, as well as
the independent monitoring and adaptive management program, are to be funded
entirely by the permittee, Southern California Edison, as a condition of the
permit.

Background

In 1974, the California Coastal Zone Conservation Commission (now, the
California Coastal Commission) authorized construction of Southern California
Edison's and San Diego Gas and Electric's Units 2 and 3 of the San Onofre
Nuclear Generating Station (SONGS) in northern San Diego County. At the time
of the approval, the Commission lacked sufficient information on the nature

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
January 14, 1992
Page 2

and extent of the impact of the power plant operations on the marine
environment. To address this lack of information, the permit (No. 183-73)
established a Marine Review Committee (MRC) to study the effects of Units 2
and 3 on the marine environment.

Following submission of the MRC's final report to the Coastal Commission, on
July 16, 1991, the Commission adopted a resolution to further condition the
permit. Three new mitigation projects to address impacts to the marine en-
vironment that the MRC found to be caused by the operations of Units 2 and 3
of SONGS were required: wetlands restoration to compensate for fish impacts,
behavioral barriers to reduce fish impingement losses, and artificial reef
construction to reduce lost resources at the San Onofre Kelp Bed.

Commission Responsibilities

The permittee has the primary responsibilities for designing and implementing
the three mitigation projects; however, due to the uncertainty of whether the
mitigation projects will successfully meet performance standards identified in
the permit, an independent third party monitoring, management and remediation
program, with Commission oversight, is also required. This "adaptive manage-
ment" program will improve the likelihood of success by monitoring and, on the
basis of the data collected, regularly evaluating the progress of the mitigã-
tion projects and determining remedial steps, if necessary.

The Commission retains responsibility for approving the restoration and
construction plans, and for determining if the goals specified in the ap-
proved plans and the performance standards contained in the permit are being
achieved. If the mitigation projects are not meeting these goals and stan-
dards, the Commission will require remedial measures. The Commission will
convene an interagency Wetland Advisory Panel and a Scientific Advisory Panel
to assist in implementing the permit conditions. The permittee is required by
the permit to provide all funding necessary for the monitoring and management
program and the Commission's oversight responsibilities.

In order to carry out the monitoring and management program, the Commission
intends to retain at least two primary consultants to provide the scientific
and technical expertise needed to oversee the mitigation projects and other
consultants and contractors needed to implement the monitoring studies, using
funds provided by the permittee. The Commission envisions entering into an
agreement with an appropriate foundation or nonprofit organization which would
then contract for necessary personnel and services, subject to the Commis-
sion's direction and approval (see "Administrative Structure," below).

The consultant scientists would work at the Commission's headquarters under
the supervision of the Commission's Manager and Deputy Manager of the Energy
and Ocean Resources Unit. Major responsibilities would include:

(1) planning (consultation on and review of site assessments, designs,
restoration and construction plans, coordination and evaluation of peer review
and review by the wetlands and scientific advisory panels);

The image appears to be a heavily faded or low-contrast scan of a document. While I can see there is text present throughout the page, it is too faint and illegible for me to accurately transcribe the specific words and content with confidence. The text appears to be formatted in multiple paragraphs, but the individual characters are not clear enough to read reliably.

To provide you with an accurate transcription following your rules, I would need a clearer version of this document.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
January 14, 1992
Page 3


     (2) monitoring studies (development of independent monitoring programs for
the wetlands restoration, reef construction and barrier devices projects,
oversight of monitoring contractors, participation in field work, analysis and
interpretation of data, coordination of advisory panels and external peer
review, evaluation of analyses and conclusions of reviewers, development of
recommendations to the Commission);

     (3) program evaluation (conducting an annual meeting for Commission staff
to review mitigation project activities, coordination of external review,
evaluation of analyses and conclusions with respect to the achievement of
performance standards and remedial measures which might be required, advice to
the Executive Director on whether performance standards are being met, recom-
mendations to the Commission for remediation); and

     (4) administration (development of biennial work programs and budgets,
preparation of Requests for Proposals for monitoring studies, coordination of
review of contract proposals, recommendations to the Executive Director on the
contractor selection, maintenance of scientific data).

Administrative Structure

     In order to accomplish the monitoring, management and oversight responsibi-
lities under this permit, an appropriate mechanism for transferring funds from
the permittee must be established. The Commission envisions a structure in
which it would enter into two separate Memoranda of Agreement. The first
would be an agreement with an appropriate foundation or nonprofit organi-
zation. This MOA would establish the terms under which the foundation or
nonprofit would provide services to the Commission. These services would
include (1) contracting for consultants and other contractors, subject to the
Commission's direction and approval, needed to carry out the above tasks and
(2) establishing a special deposit account to receive funds from the permittee
to cover the costs of those tasks. Funds would be drawn from the special
account only upon the authorization of the Executive Director of the
Commission.

     A separate MOA would be executed between the Commission and the permittee.
This MOA would establish the work program and budget for the SONGS monitoring,
management and oversight program and would direct the permittee to deposit the
necessary funds in the account established in the Commission's MOA with a
foundation or nonprofit organization.

     The proposed administrative structure would meet the Commission's needs. The
Commission has the authority to contract for personnel and services necessary
to carry out its oversight, monitoring and management functions for the SONGS
mitigation projects. If the Commission contracts with an appropriate foundation
or nonprofit, the administrative function would be consolidated into one
"master" agreement, which would maximize the efficiency of the program and
simplify fund transfers and other administrative procedures, thus minimizing
the overall costs to the permittee. Further, the consolidation would enable
the Commission to respond quickly to unforeseen circumstances, such as the
need for additional studies or research, and provide adequate flexibility to
retain appropriate consultants and contractors.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
January 14, 1992
Page 4


The Commission is currently investigating appropriate third party entities
with which to enter into the master agreement. One entity which has expressed
interest, and with which the Commission has worked on federally-funded
projects in the Tijuana River National Estuarine Research Reserve, is the San
Diego State University Foundation. The Foundation is a nonprofit corporation
designated as an auxiliary organization in the Education Code of the State of
California. The Foundation annually administers over $50 million; it enables
University faculty to engage in sponsored research and education programs,
offers a vehicle to accept, invest and expand donated monies, and is the legal
"umbrella" under which many community-oriented activities operate. Because of
the foundation's experience and mission, it appears that it might be an appro-
priate entity to administer the monitoring and management program for the
Commission.

Conclusion

In order that the permittee will not be considered a "source of income" under
Government Code section 87103.6 to the consultants and contractors retained by
the Commission, even though the permittee pays the fees for employing them, we
understand that the Commission, and not the permittee, must control the flow
of income to and the decisionmaking of the consultants and contractors. We
believe that the arrangement described above would provide sufficient agency
control over the consultants' monitoring and management activities for the
mitigation projects to meet the requirements of Government Code section
87103.6 and the FPPC's regulations. Under the "master" agreement, the Com-
mission would retain authority to determine which consultants and contractors
are hired, direct the work and evaluate the performance of the consultants and
contractors, and establish the amount of compensation paid. The consultants
and contractors would be paid from a fund over which the permittee exercises
no control or authority; funds would be drawn from the account only upon the
authorization of the Commission.

The MOA between the Commission and the foundation or nonprofit would be
separate from the MOA between the Commission and the permittee, and it would
include terms which specify that compensation paid to the consultants and
contractors is dependent upon completion of the work and not dependent upon
any agency action pertaining to the mitigation projects. Similarly, the MOA
between the Commission and the permittee would include terms which specify the
amounts and schedule of payments to the account and specify that the payment
of any mitigation fees as required by the permit conditions is not contingent
on the hiring of any specific person or consultant, is not dependent upon the
outcome of the consultants' work or their recommendations, and is not depen-
dent upon any further agency action.

Thus, we believe that the proposed administrative structure meets the intended
purpose of the FPPC's regulation as well as Government Code section 87103.6,
and creates an adequate buffer between the permittee, whose mitigation fees
would pay for the employment of the consultants and contractors, and the con-
sultants and contractors, whose work would consist of reviewing and monitoring
the permittee's mitigation projects.


To provide you with an accurate transcription following your rules, I would need a higher contrast or clearer version of this document image.

Mr. Scott Hallabrin
January 14, 1992
Page 5


Please advise us at your earliest possible convenience whether the proposed
administrative structure for the monitoring and management program for the
SONGS marine resource mitigation projects is acceptable. Southern California
Edison has submitted its first report in accordance with the permit condi-
tions, and we need to proceed expeditiously to hire the scientific consultants
in order for the mitigation projects to remain on schedule. If you have any
questions, please feel free to call Dorothy Dickey of my staff at (415)
904-5220.

Very truly yours,

[handwritten signature]
Peter M. Douglas
Executive Director

5363N
